speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like there, where you grew up in Florence that made you want to, uh, near Florence and made you want to leave?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, I, I, no, I didn't grow up in that in the city. Yeah. It was 40 miles straight west of Florence. But halfway between Florence and the west coast, just exactly halfway, nice little town. They call it Montecatini, but we live a mile and a quarter out of town in a little acreage there. I don't know, probably eight or ten acres, I would say. But we had the landlord, dammit, and that was rough. You know, half went to the landlord. Everything was razed, and probably more sometimes. So you could have barely, you couldn't even make it live. No, no, no, no. It was really rough, and that's why my oldest brother did now.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He threw a digger out of the army. He came over, and then a sister and a brother follow him two years. No, I think his brother is over here in the one actually now, next to me. He left, he left home. He's 13 and a half years old.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He came over with, what would you call a passport?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: A passport that wasn't his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Forged passport?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, it would have been somebody else. A guy was old enough and small, even though he got the passport, so he took his name and passport and he came over. So when he applied for citizenship, he had to tell them the whole thing, what had happened, because anxious to come over, but he couldn't. He was too young, but he was well developed and strong and big. They made it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they didn't punish him for that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, no. They used to let him go to the regular, yeah, people like anybody else, take out the first paper, and then he spent, I think he was in Alaska then when he became citizen. He went to Alaska when the sawmill opened up and he was shut down, no hope of starting up, so he went to Alaska.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when these brothers and your sister came over, where did they go when they first came to the States?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Wilson Creek.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Wilson Creek. Wilson Creek was a railroad town, a library town, a lot of work. It was a division point on the railroad.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You know what I mean? You understand what I mean?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Freight, not passengers, it was a division point for freight. All the freight, those days, they only traveled 15, 20 miles an hour and lots of traffic on the railroad then.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: When they got 110 miles out of Spokane, there was this little town, they had the rate hours, see? Oh, did they, what did they do on the railroad then? What would they do?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, some of them they owned a section, but this brother that I got over here now, he was a car inspector right in Wilson Creek.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He passed in your freight, they all inspected it, they wouldn't go by Wilson Creek until all the, what do you call it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They call them hot bucks, you know, when they start getting hot, you know, they had to drink the dope and oil and then sometimes they drink the plate. Those days they didn't make, I mean, freight cars like they do now. See them on the railroad, they just, there you go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when, after the war there in Italy, it was pretty rough, wasn't it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, it was rough during the war too, I'm telling you.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: There's no question about it, we didn't have too much to eat. Everything was rationing, you know, and even though we are out there on a neck ridge of our hackery, the landlord took his share first.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did that mean, like what did you eat mostly then?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, it's mostly, what do we call it, pasta, you know, anything made of such as macaroni and spaghetti and beans, a lot of beans and greens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to go in the army there?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, that's why I'm over here. Not only I was looking for work, but I was due to go in the army within eight or nine months. But, boy, I didn't like that. I seen it after the war, I just didn't want to go into the army.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I heard a lot of people that were going in were just going up the lines getting shot and that was it.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: What the hell, the army is just some ruler decided to go to war, that's all there is to it. So, I wasn't, in those days, this country didn't have no draft, you know, just volunteer army. I didn't, that's, well, but over there we didn't. So, I, it was a kind of a thing that I was late to go to, you know, to get a passport. But, I had a cousin that worked for the county, you know, in Elfs. And he said, I think I can get you through. He told me just what to do, you know, and sure enough, he sent me to the city, 25 miles west, where there was, what would you call it, over here, you go to Spokane to pass that physical for the army. Well, over there was the city and he got me some paper, got me started, and got a physical examination to see if I was fit to go to the army. And before I was 25, I would come back, I signed an affidavit, and if I was over, passed the examination, it was kind of a doubt. I think, well, the guy told me what to do, to breathe hard, you know, and fully adopt, you know, on my heart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do to do that? How did you do that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, you know, you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You do, what do you do, jump around, run around?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, and then if you started breathing hard, your heart started beating faster, you see. Well, anyway, okay, you signed an affidavit. Before you're 25, you come back here and pass another examination and see where you were, where it would let you stay over there. But I had an excuse, you know, I had a sister and three brothers by then over here, you see, and it made it a little easier. But unless I had this cousin working in the county, I would have been, no, no, I wouldn't know what to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what year was this?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: 1920. 1920, I think in April, yeah, because we... on Easter, we were out in the middle of the ocean, Easter Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go with friends? Did you go with friends or by yourself?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, there were a few friends, yeah, not only friends, I had a... there was a girl over there that one of my brothers had gone with her. But he came out of the army too and boy, he beat it and then sent for her and she was alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was coming to marry him? Hmm?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah, and they got married the first day I was there. She got over there, got married, unafraid of. They had a nice little girl, you know, she had played with her a lot, but his wife didn't like it over there. After three years, he had to go back with her. Took the kid, but I mean, to see that little baby go back there. But when she was 20, she married a guy and he had some relation in this country, Pittsburgh, and she decided to come over. And at the age of 20, I couldn't tell you which, but one of the two, in the age of 20 or 21, she had a decision to make, whether she wanted to stay there and become an Italian citizen or come back here and be an American citizen. So she came back and the immigration people, they wrote me quite a few times. They wanted a lot of information and finally I couldn't tell them too much, you know, after all my knowledge limited. But finally I happened to think this kid was baptized in Ephrata. So I wrote to them and told them to write at Ephrata, Grand County, you know, and the record was still there and they did. That was okay then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't the mother like it here?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, she couldn't talk, she didn't know anybody, she just got disgusted right away. And it is kind of rough for a young, yeah, I talked her out in the county, I don't know why someone is dating her. When you were young, you wanted to chase her around and that was out. Well were there other Italian women there that she could... Well, my sister was there, yeah, but you know, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: See, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I guess I'd heard that a lot of, most people that come over, they left their wives behind or their bachelors, isn't that true?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well of course there was a, in those days, there was before your time, but there was a time when a guy could come over, you know, and work at least seven or eight months in a railroad. And you had to talk her out, the railroad was kind of a rough business, especially unless you had some relation up the tongue. You live in a bunk house, you know, on a railroad car, and you do your own cooking, your own washing, and it's not very pleasant. But a lot of those guys that did come over, and then they sent their wife back, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This living in the bunk house, that business, that's what you've done when you come over here, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No I didn't because I had a sister. She was well established, and her husband had a pretty good job too. And they had a nice home right in town, right in Wolfson Creek, nice home. So I stayed there, and you know, she done the washing, and I slept, and I ate there, and paid room and board to them. But there was, you know, more like a home and living on a bunkhouse and a railroad, you never get to talk to anybody, and you don't know anybody, and no entertainment of any kind. You can't go and do anything.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You know, you fucked yourself when you were 20 years old, and lived that kind of life for 2 or 3 years, you'd get disgusted, wouldn't you?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well that'd be tough. It would be tough, but that's tough enough. To be doing that, when those guys, not to have anybody to cook for them, you know, like in the logging camps, they always had somebody a good cook. Well in logging camps, that's different. I spent a year in a logging camp. I'm glad you mentioned that. Everybody, they are the best people there is, you know, those lumberjacks. I don't know about now, but in those days, the real lumberjacks, they were good people. They would play cards every night, crib, you know what that is, crib, play cribs every night, they would talk, everybody would have some stories to tell. Oh heck, time went fast, you know. The logging camp beside, by the time I got into the logging camp, those camps, they were clean. You had the clean sheets every week, company furnish sheets, you know. You had the hot and cold water for a shower, but two years before that, the Wobblies got the wood on strike, and if it wasn't for the Wobblies, they'd be like that again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's right, the Wobblies did it, didn't they?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They did the Wobblies, in fact, I have a neighbor of yours, one of the best friends of yours, he's been a vice president, retired now, for the PFI, you know, they started. Yeah, sure. He's one of my best friends, and he admits, you know, Wobblies, what made him clean out the camp, but they were for lights and everything else.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: That's what I've heard, I've heard the Wobblies did the job. Wobblies, Wobblies had done it, there's no question about it, everybody told me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of strike was it, in this area where you were, what was the camp that you were in?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, up there was the camp, hey, by that time, camp, hey, up in Elk River, you know, out of Elk River, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they said, when they talked about that strike, did they say they shot everything down?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah, oh, you bet, you bet, no, no, the lumberjacks usually, they stick pretty much together, it isn't like when we went on strike over here in the sawmill, you had so many scabs that they finally lost the strike, you see. But the, the lumberjacks, I seen those guys, in those days, I used to, you know, I was single, went to Spokane once in a while, go down on French Avenue, they used to call it Skateaway, and you see that wobbling away, you know. But they would stop a guy, say, brother, I need a drink, nobody ever refused him, everybody would give him 50 cents or a dollar, you bet.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And there was those kind of people, you know. They took care of their own. You bet, you bet, you bet they did, yeah. In fact, they were pretty well trusted, too, there was a, in order to get into Elk River, you had to go to St. Mary's on an air walk, you know, and then from St. Mary's, they took a little branch line, there was a passenger train going into Elk River, I think around 65, 70 miles, I don't know. They made a run, you see, into Elk River and back. But there was a conductor there, the only conductor I know, and the lumberjack coming, coming from Spokane, going from, going up to Elk River, or stop at Bovill, or Clark, you know, and they were all, they knew each other from 100 miles around. And they would never, I don't have any ticket, no money, but I'll pay you. And the conductor would never throw them out, but he said he never lost a penny.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You bet, and something else, I put a year in it, and the trouble was that when I got ready to go back to work, the camps, they were still shut down, and I wasn't used to a loop and a run, so I went to work in South Melton. But otherwise, I want to go back to work so bad. You get so that you like that kind of work, you like the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The camps were shut down because of the weather?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, the eye is usually, I seen in those days, of course, those days you had a horse. That's what you didn't have, it means you had it today. Everything was cross cut in a horse to schedule logs, you know, and it got too soft, I used to can't, I seen the time, when people can, they're looking for a skinner, we used to call him. Two soy, four soy, job do at the fort. Oh yes, many, many places, it was still a little wet after the fort. Yeah, it was a short season. Yeah, it was a season of work, that's all it was, that's why the lumberjack, he never found one that had a wife, you know, they were all single guys. They blow in in Spokane. They went to Spokane with a couple hundred bucks, two hundred bucks in those days, it was a lot of money, two, three hundred bucks. In about a week or two weeks time, they come back broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And drink, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Drink in it, you go to four houses. Moonshine, see those days, there's nothing but moonshine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then there were all the prostitutes, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah, oh yeah, and some smart girl, they would get a hold of one of those guys, stay with him, you know, and probably took all his money away first and say, yeah, let me, because you'll get drunk, and a lot of them do that, you know. And some of those prostitutes, they would be honest enough, you know, to, not to drip too much, but they all got their,

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: they got pretty well paid for their pussy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These guys though, the funny thing about them, one thing, you look back at it, you know, it seems like when you blow in, man, a guy would make a lot of money and that's all gone, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, it was, you don't have a wife, you don't have a family, no responsibility, no nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you get so that you've been broke once or twice, but you never suffer because somebody carry you along, see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You probably repaid him, oh yeah, you've done the same thing for somebody else, but it's unbelievable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the lumberjack, how they stuck together, and they understood it, if a guy was drunk, they knew how he went broke, see? So you didn't have, did you have to know the guy very good, I mean another lumberjack?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh, if you're a lumberjack, you could spot a lumberjack right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's that? How can you spot it? Because the way he was dressed?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The way they talk, they all had that dirty word in their mouth, you know, never open their mouth unless they damn it or fuck you, you know, something like that. That's the way they talk. There was no place for a lady to be around, see?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you really like the class of people that were lumberjacks better than the guys that were the sawmillers?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yes, I did, there's no question about it, in fact I could tell you that I've been beat out of a few hundred bucks, very few too, but never from a lumberjack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the difference between them guys and like the sawmillers, the guys in the sawmill? Well, it's a different, it's a different breed of a people, the sawmill, you have a family, you go home, you know, to your family every day, but the lumberjack is a different guy, see? He didn't have that responsibility, and that's why they live different, but they were honest, see? What did you do when you were working in the woods, the lumberjack? Well, of course, when I went in the woods I saw, saw a drip of wood, see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: So much a thousand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was you and your brother? Yeah, yeah, yeah, then you see the, on those days, what we do, we went to any camp, if they didn't have a job right there, they'll hold you, stay here, you know, and find somebody, you know?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And then it takes, most of the time it takes two or four, four guys to get what they call a strip of the timber to log the rock, bring them down to the railroad.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In those days, they log, just like I said, they log different, they build a railroad into the woods, see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah! Just like I said, when we went up in the woods, you know, we went in there, and the foreman said, wait a day or two, and we stay over here, you know, and we wait in there, and sure enough, it was only a day and a half or so that here comes a couple guys, so the four would go together.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: One was what they call a swamper, and one a teamster, a skinner, see? And I and my brother were the sawyer, we saw the logs, 16 foot length, or if we can make any, and the swamper would see that the guy with the team, he'd have a load of them ready to take along, you see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But then, when we got halfway through our job,

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: those guys, they were both Frenchmen, and they started to fight among themselves, so one pull off and went, you see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And in that round, we had so damn much money accumulated, because, you see, we were getting just the wage, I think it was $3 .40, $3 .50 a day. Then you get a bonus.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And the bonus, that was cap, you see, but we had to finish our job in order to collect it, you see? The strip was worth so much, a thousand, see? You get the bonus on every thousand, right? And the bonus on every thousand, it ended the job, you see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: It ended the job, they come out, the foreman would come out and look at the points, see if it was okay, then they pay you off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened when he left? Then we handled between the three of us, you see? And we were taking somebody else in, probably the guy would have gained $150, $200, you see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: So we done it, also.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you handle it, the three of you?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The three of us, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you handle it? Just three of you?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, we did it this way.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In order to fix the road, this kid's road, and this and that, sometimes we go out after supper, see? And do a little bit of a job, you know? Because they won't let you keep the team out, you know, after eight hours, see? But you could go out and fix your trailer, fix your, you know?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Or if you had a bad tree that was leaning in a different way and you couldn't, took a long time to push it, just what he wanted to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did not have to suffer?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you have to work really hard on that street?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, it was hard until you got on to it.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: It's a young punk of a kid, you know?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But I stayed away with it. We done it. In fact, we built a little reputation after, you know, for doing it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean for doing just the three of you?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you join the IWW when you came?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, no. In fact, they didn't bother you too much, then either. If they had come around, everybody would have it. But by then, they didn't bother you. The IWW, the poor last strike, about then, that was it. And I don't know. I couldn't tell them too much about that because, just like I said, they didn't bother us. But we could hear, and I kind of think they left you alone for a year or so.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They want to, when you join the IWW, they want to be sure that you mean it, you know?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And stay with it. I kind of think that's the way I got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what year was it that you were in the woods there?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In 24. That was 24. 24.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, well, going back a little before that, when you first came, when you first come here from Italy, when you first got out there, how did you get? Did you take the train all the way out?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, I did.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In fact, I stopped in Chicago. Of course, you know, by that time, I was told just what to do, right to my brother. And he had grown kids. He had married a widow. And they told me what to do, so I stopped in Chicago. I knew some people there. And they found a guy that was coming into Spokane.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: So, I went into St. Paul with this guy, and they really wrote, they gave me a pass to come out west. I see, it's pretty nice, but they did on it right along. Those days, they did want to build the West anyhow.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Besides, I knew a damn mother that couldn't go out and do anything else but railroad, see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And I did. I went out on the railroad in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you knew railroad was the only job you could do? Well, what else could you do when you can't talk? And who would hire you?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: See, even in a saw mill, unless you know, you can talk a little bit, or the former can defend yourself. But in the railroad, it's just one job, and after one day or two, you're on your own.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But you'd never done that back in Italy. That kind of works. No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was it like with the language, to not know the English? Was that hard at first?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, yeah, but what I mean, but not to work, because like I said, it's a job that you learn so quick. You see, the boss will take you on a job, and then you know what to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it? What was it that you done on that job?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh, bad ties. He marked a bad one to pull out and replace with a new one. See, you line up the track, well, seven or eight guys, you know what to do there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the guys on that crew Italian?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? You couldn't buy your own cigarette?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, gosh, damn it, finally, it got so that I listened to those guys, you know, and they used to say, pronounced almost the same, you see. So, cigarette, cigarette, so I...

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But they would say, what kind, you know, well, I had an idea about the... See, in those days, it was a camel chest, the field, the lucky strike. They are very seldom see any other brand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the work itself? Was it hard, was that real hard work?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, it's not easy work, you know. It's plenty hard, yes, you bet. It's thick and shovel, you know, and it's enough for a sissy.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But, what the hell, you're over here to work. But the one guy on his 20, you know, 19 and 20, after all, once he get broke in on the job, it's not too bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was your brother coming along, your other brothers and sisters that were here, were they learning the language fast?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, well, they used to like it, but they knew the language pretty good, except this one over here, you know, when he hit nuts. Because he came over and told you 13 and a half years old.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But, you know, I was kind of... See, then from Wilson Creek, I had a brother, the oldest brother, he was in Montana, east of L .A .V., 30 miles. And he wanted me to go over there, work for him, he was a former. And once in a while I go over there, and then I stay there, and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of format?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Just a section format.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And I told him once, I said, is there any place that I can get a newspaper, you know, that I can read it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And he pointed, Spokesman Review, and said, yeah, I can. He said, keep on looking at it, don't look at it, if you can read it, look at it. And that's it. You know, he said, you gotta learn English sometimes.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, you spent most of your time with the family, you probably... Well, I was young. You've been speaking Italian, you wouldn't be speaking English.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I was like, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Otherwise, what the hell could you do? Go out in a goddamn bunkhouse and do your own cooking?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I don't like that. So I stay with my brother, he was married, and had family there, and... He paid for the room there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, do you remember when you were first here, and like over there, ever having a hard time because you were a foreigner?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Because you didn't speak... I mean, people who held it against you because you... Well, sure, there's no question about it. You're kinda up against it all the time. See, you're a handicap. You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever get in fights over that, somebody giving you a hard time?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, no, I know it, even when I first started talking, you know, sometimes I'd say, kinda slap, you know, they would laugh, I'd let them laugh. You'd be surprised how many times I'd laugh at some of those guys trying to say something. So, no, I didn't let that bother me too much.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But you see, I was right, I'm 25 years old before I even started going out with any girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that, in Elk River?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You're... was that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: when you started going out with girls at Elk River, who were they?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Were they girls that were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: whose parents?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, they were English, they were born over here.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But, you know, not too even to a show here and there, you know. In those days, I didn't go to dance as much because I just, you know, kinda...

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: When you don't speak the language, you're good, you know, you kinda hold yourself back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these local girls, though, they were like the Elk River girls, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They grew up... Yeah, they were Elk River girls, oh yeah, some of them, but still here is one.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Married again, retired, she's retired over here, I went to a show quite a few times with her and... I see her a few times, but, you know, never see her. When I finally got serious with one, she was from Derry, married her, but she was great, she turned out to be... So, good, they gave me a couple of kids and then I had to kick it out. That was rough! Yeah. That was rough, you bet. And that was in the 30s, in the early 30s. In the Depression. Yeah, that's correct. But by that time I was over here in this place, not in this place, I had bought in those two weeks of LA and over here, you know, two bits down, two bits, money, you know. Live in the corner, we tore down an old house up in Elk River. I bought five room house, bath and everything for $100 and tore it down. Try to rebuild it over here, but as soon as I got the roof on, I moved in.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Those days, they were bad ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? Didn't the house come back together right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, no, they used to tore it down apart, piece by piece, you know.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But all the windows and doors and bathroom fixture, you know, a lot of that stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you could use that again?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah, reuse that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't some people move the whole darn house out?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, no, it's pretty hard to do that, you know. In those days, especially, you don't have the roads, you don't have the means of moving them. So, a lot of house were moved, alright, but just like I did.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The bigger house, can we see it from here?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Now we got to know the bigger two -story house over here. It was moved too. There you can see, part of the garage. If the windows worked over here, we could see the bathroom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you decided to leave and to come to Elk River area with your brother, what kind of strike was that? Had you gone on strike too, on the section?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, this wasn't on the section. I told you, he was a car inspector. Yeah, a car inspector went on strike and they kind of lost out on the strike. And it probably was a good move for me to go with him then, see. And I finally ended up over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he lose his job in that strike?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the union, I wondered, at that time?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, the union in those days, of course, they still got them now too.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Under the, it was kind of a system, you had the engineer, you had the fireman, you had the conductor, you had the brickman, you had the section man, you had the car inspector, you had so many different branches into the union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, they're all different, huh?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You see, what they call a craft union. Craft union, yeah. Sure, that's what we had over here in the Sun Mill, under the F of L. And finally we organized the CIO. The CIO is the industrial union, that's everybody. That's right, that's right, takes everybody belong to the union. That works better. Well, it sure works better, you bet, sure. Yeah, yeah, yeah, that was the outcome. That was, just like I said, that was probably for the best because I ended up with a pretty fair job over here. I got to be a pretty good grader, lumber grader, finally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get into the mill? Was there a lot of jobs at the time, openings in the mill?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no. Elk River.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Elk River, of course, in those days was a tandelion and help, good help especially, was hard to get. You know, so there was no problem with going up there and get a job in the Sun Mill. So, then when the Sun Mill was shut down, of course, by that time it was married, family, then they shut down the downtown thing and never started anymore. But there was a superintendent down here, the yard superintendent, and over here in the western, and he knew that there were a lot of people up there in Elk River yet, you know, that they work in the Sun Mill. They understood the number, they knew what they were doing, and rather than hire a new guy, he would call those guys. Not only, it was kind of an obligation to the company to get some of those guys, because it was the same company. Yeah, they laid them all off. So, it was on the 4th of July, or on the 5th of July, in 33, in the meantime there was quite a few guys over here already from Elk River. And this guy had told me about me and another guy, so they called us and said to come down. We came down, get a job, all right. Anyway, six hours a day, two days a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Doing what? What were you doing?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, a number, just anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In 33.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two days a week, huh?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Two days a week, three days a week, you go to work, 8 o 'clock, sometime, 11 o 'clock, well that's all for the day. One department shut down, when we come back we'll call you. I don't have any phone, but I couldn't afford it. Somebody else is some neighbor. And then, try to catch a ride with somebody all the time. There was a five, one guy would pick us up, you know, and before, and the driver, many times two or three of us lose our ride. The driver, he was a manorilla driver, maybe get to work till five that day. Or maybe sometime, it was his turn to go home early, and we had to work another hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you do then if you didn't have a ride?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Walk.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I went two years without an automobile. I couldn't buy one. Walk from the sawmill over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far would that be now?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Six miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Each way? For how much money?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You were making it... I was making the ride on an average rate. Twenty, twenty -one, two dollars for two weeks of work, every payday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you live on that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No. But I got a print with a lot of those guys. One guy is still up here now, Harry Heisman. Most of them are dead now, but they had big gardens. They were raising gardens, you know, produced to sell, fruit, cherry, peaches, anything. Whenever there was nothing else to do, I was at home, I used to go over and help those guys. I would get produced, fruit for free. And anytime I had a cherry tree that was dying out, I could have it if I dug it by the roots for my wood.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Rough. Those are rough. Don't tell me it was rough. It was rough. You're telling me. I know. It was rough. There's no question about it. But some of those guys, they don't believe it. But I made it. I paid my bills. Took a long time to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that when you and your wife, they're split up?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, I had it pretty rough. Then I had to have a housekeeper pay the housekeeper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you keep the kids?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah, I kept the kids. Oh yeah. Oh yeah, the neighbors would have hung me if I hadn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The neighbors?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah, all the neighbors stuck up for me. They helped me out some. That way, you know.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: She must not have been much of a mother then for the kids. No, no.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They ended the kids. But in those days, the youngest kids, they showed them quite five. And they had one six. They didn't rely. They thought that was a mean daddy that they had.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: So then you kind of raised them kids yourself. Oh yeah, sure.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Sure. You bet. And then, we're not funny. I married this girl over here. We get along fine. We've been married almost 30 years now. We have a girl, 25 years old. She's not married yet. She's got a good job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it for you to kind of take care of the kids? Was that hard to kind of be a mother to them too?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, sure. Of course. Like I said, I had pretty good neighbors. One neighbor lived in this double house over here.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Both are dead now. They were wonderful people to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They took care?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, oh yeah. I lived between them and the housekeeper. But it was rough. I had the debts to pay.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You had the housekeeper right during the Depression then. You had her in 1933. Sure, sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well listen, Elk River, when you were there, what was the town like in the 20s? Was it a booming town?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: It was. Like I said, it was a wonderful little town. Everybody had money because everybody worked. It was a big town. It wasn't a guy that didn't have a job. A few bombs didn't even show up those days.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: You know those days that you go on the mainline railroad?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Bumby, you see them go by by the hundreds, you know. But not in Elk River. Everybody worked there. Everybody had money. Everybody's happy. Saturday night, we'd have a dance. Picture show. That was it. In the wintertime, you couldn't go on a town with a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that meant during the winter, was there a lot of socialite in the town? Right in the town?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, yes. Dances. You try the same picture show. That's about all. I heard that they shut down the picture show on Sundays in Elk River. Well, of course, once in a while you get to see what they call the one horse of town.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The guy that runs the song mail with the big balls, you know. He wants to be elected mayor of the town. He wants to run the school board. He wants to run not only the song mail, he wants to run the town too.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, you heard of the one horse of town, haven't you?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, there was one horse of town. But, like I said, nobody minded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Bloom?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Bloom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that his idea to shut down a movie site or something?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I heard it was some of the church people that got... Well, even the religious man too, see.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, Andrew Bloom. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of boss was he?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, of course, he didn't have nothing to do with the man. He was a general superintendent. But, well, the guy, he couldn't talk English better than I can now. Hell, not even half the business. But, well, I don't know what I should say. But he was a timber cruiser by trade, you know. He made a living. He was a timber cruiser. And in those days, everybody, they could steal from Uncle Sam with his donut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, do you get what I mean?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The sole cruiser got to know how many million feet of a timber went into the potlatch for nothing. So, he got a good job. Not that he knew how to run the saw mill.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But don't repeat it. No, he helped him set up. Well, he got it. Sure, sure. Well, I've heard the same thing about Deere in potlatch. Oh, yeah, well, sure.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: It's a, well, there is just as much good work than there is today.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Politics is what I have here. And you had all that, and there was all that timber just sitting there, you know. Nobody had used it or nothing.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, these people over here, I told you they got to be my best friend during the Depression. The old man, he was a, he ran the first camp for the original warehouses. He ran the first camp. And he said, boy, did we get the timber. The warehouses in those days, the four sheriffs were different. A guy would go in there and say, I want a strip of a timber. And he'd buy a section of a timber. But he don't buy it over here handy. He go two, three miles back over here. That's the timber I want. And then he gets it, he get to cut the right -of -way for nothing. He got to get into it.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And this guy, Hamley is his name, he said, we got more timber for free than what I bought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that in Idaho here?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, Croquet. Oh, Minnesota, yeah. Croquet, Minnesota. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. But then, the call, of course, they came to our village with Hamley, this guy.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But he had to, he used to tell me a story. When he first, he first opened up this camp, he enjoy Hamley of course, to run it. And one day, it was raining. Here comes the old man over the house himself, up the camp. He said, he got inside, he started looking out, he said, Joe, what a day for the horses. What a day for the horses. He said, he kept it up for about an hour. Finally said, Joe, go tell those guys to bring the horses in. He was feeling so annoyed for the horses.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, I should talk, I should tell those. Oh, no, that's okay. But don't go and repeat it. No, no, well, you know, that kind of story, I heard stories about old man Weyerhaeuser before, you know. Well, that's the way he got started, by stealing most of the water. That's what everybody says, that's nothing new. Well,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: in Elk River, when you were there, were there many other guys that were Italian and working in the Sommeville?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, I would say, well, there was quite a bunch of them. There was one bunch that came right from the border between Austria and Italy.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And I couldn't tell whether they were Italian, they talked both language.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Italian and German, too?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, they were Austrian, yeah. And they talked both language, you know. So, there is one up here now, still alive, I didn't say a lot. He was from Venice. There was two, three families from Venice. The Nenders, probably five, six or seven of those guys from way up north, right up on the line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many were families were there? There were some with families, some single?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: There were some married families, I would say probably five or six, seven.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That had families?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, yeah. And then there was more, there were some more guys that just by themselves. Yeah, a single guy, yeah. There was a boat in the house, a woman ran a boat in the house. She had lost her husband and she had two kids. One was named John, the other one Louis. And John finally went to Spokane, just before somebody was shot down up on the river. And he went into the freight business. He started in with an old beat up truck and he ended up with the United, owning the United freight line. No time at all, got to be a multimillionaire. And I've seen the day that I go in for a bit to go to a dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now you see how it goes? Is that luck?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, there was luck, and he had guts too. He had guts and he had the mama to back him up. Mama, even the depression didn't hold her too much because she kept her money. So she had enough money. He started out with a service station in Spokane and finally bought himself an old beat up truck. And he got to run from Seattle Portland to Spokane. But he got one of those big outfits that was the Monkey Ward, the Sears, or some of those big chainsaws. He got to do all the hauling. And then he had it on the truck and the first thing he know, he got himself a fleet of trucks. And then he took his brother in as a farmer and held the fire spread out to Alaska. Finally, it was sold to United Buckingham.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Do you ever see that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Unite Buckingham now. On the trucks. For a while, it was just United. Buckingham was by itself. But it was sold out and I think now probably it's run by Buckingham. But to me, I remember many times he used to say, Johnny, come on. Because he always had a narrow bill. Never enough money to put in. Mama, he worked just like I did. You know, down in the saw mill, the truck went to Mama and then Mama gave him a dollar back. Bought the car alright, but kept him down. So he invited me to go to Yale or there to Moscow or some place, you know, because he knew that I had a dollar in my pocket. And then, first thing you know, the guy is a multimillionaire and I'm a poor guy trying to do a fiddle. That's what most of us are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you started in the mill, what did you start doing in your first job?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I looked at numbers.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In those days, I had lots of handwork, hand number, you know, handling number. Especially after the drill was, most of the numbers dried out in the yard. And I and my brother, when I was in Jupu, worked with all the Jupu.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that pretty good money?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Very, didn't wage, you know, double our wage, see. In those days, I think wage was 40 cents an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you could make 80 cents an hour loading?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you would just work eight hours?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, well, of course, yeah, just about eight hours and then never any more than that. We ate probably lunch and go right back to work, but then we all quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like with the guys in the mill? Was there much, the guys, how did they get along? Did they tease each other a lot, joking?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, yeah, of course, you couldn't talk to the guy in the mill. In the mill, it never worked. It was on the planter side, on the lumber, you know, the dry lumber side. See, and they found in the sawmill, I went and put it over in the green chain in one year, and that was a pretty fair job. It paid, I think it was 50 cents an hour in those days.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: But these guys in the sawmill, like, they weren't as close as the guys were in the camps.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh no, no, no, no, no, because I told you it's a different breed of people, people, family, people, see. So they had other, you mean like maybe they had other ties, so they weren't, they didn't care about the... Well, no, everybody for themselves, you know, in other words, surely somebody down and out, they kept a collection anytime a guy threw into bits, you see. But the, but a lumberjack, if he was down and out, still he was drinking, and he wanted another drink, he didn't make any bones about it. He asked you for a drink, the price for a drink. Yeah. And he got it. You get what I mean now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah, okay, but in the mill, why weren't these guys friendly the same way?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, they were friendly, but they, but they, but they, if you were drunk and you spend all your money, hell with you, I got kids to feed, see. But the other lumberjack, he was single guy too. He didn't have any kids, he shared less than a dollar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, what about, say, the union with these guys in the sawmill? Did they care about the union?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, in those days, they didn't have any union. Finally, they came, the company union came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, was that the 4L?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh, the 4L, and I and a little young Swede, we were working on a green chain, and we decided, God damn, we wasn't going to join it. And finally, the boss come out, he said, I'm sorry, I don't want to see you go, I don't want to let you go. Both of these guys are pretty good men, but you must join the union, force us into it. Then we, when they came down over here, they didn't even ask us to join, they just started taking the money from the payroll. They was to pay for the 4L.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the 4L do anything for the men that you could see?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do good?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, they did, because I didn't lost my job, but I lost my advancement three or four times to the boss's son, to the boss's son, and also the boss's cousin. I went up to the union, oh, he said, boy, one nice thing about to belong to the union over here, he said, a guy don't have to stay on this job. If you don't like it, you can go. That's what I told you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wait a minute, so you mean that they protected your job?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Protect me in one way, if I didn't like it, I could quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's no protection, is it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They told me it was, you see, this was in Russia, they didn't hold me over here, I was free to go. The business agent and timekeeper for the union was Jerry Johnston, he was the timekeeper. The president of the union…

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Then, of course, when I started grading, I picked up the grading, and then the company started having those, what they call the students, you know, the college guy come in, they come in sometimes, they would have him in bunch, they bring eight or ten, some of the top guys, you know. Then they put one with me, one with somebody else, one with somebody else, you know, to train him, you know, so they would learn the trick of the lumber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, I trained him, show him the grade, and then once in a while the guy would say, how do you scale lumber?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, I taught him how to scale lumber. I didn't have to, but I taught him. Here comes the first year,

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: taking in inventory, couple actually shot the whole print down, those guys, they got to working, I stayed at home, and I want to work so damn badly because we wasn't working enough. So, that's how I taught those guys, they were the ones that got that extra work, not me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they working regular during that time, or did they just bring them out from Moscow to do that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: They came over here from Moscow and until they were, they spent, say, four, five, six, it depends, whatever the supervisor thought, they understand from here to another, somebody allowed in the coast, you know, to make a settlement out of them.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: That's what they were doing, and that's why I finally, when I finally joined the CIO, I stayed with the union and boy, I never missed a meeting, I got to help around in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you help, did you, when that, wait a minute, one more thing about this 4L, they didn't stop, say, another, the boss from giving a job to his son over you, I mean, what happened, did you lose?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: What else, you know, it's advanced, you take it, especially on grade, you know, almost anything, the result of that, a job that pay from two and a half to five cents an hour more, you know, they're a better job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, who gets that advance?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The boss's son, the boss's son of a bitch, the boss, you know what I mean, or the boss, maybe the guy bought him a drink, bought him a bottle of whiskey, you know, and I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's the 4L?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: 4L, they have the 4L, yes, then when the crews come along and they out -load the company union, they come back from the back door, IEU, they call it, they give you a different name, but they did that. It was the same union, it was the same… Yeah, yeah, it was the same hospital. Same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did most of the men feel the same way you did? Well, sure, but what can you do about it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you got better jobs, you did get better jobs while you were there anyway, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, after we got organized, yes, after we got organized. So you did this lumber grading you did after… Well, I was busy even before the CIO, but I never got any place, I stayed on that job, never got to advance, but soon as we got organized, I got up to the top in no time.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: And I want to tell you something else, the company didn't do that goddamn thing by advancing me rather than advancing a guy that didn't know anything, it was just because he was a boss's friend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, look, when that started, that organizing, when did it start?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was down here, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: It was down here, yeah, oh yes, oh yes, oh yes. Wildcat strike or nothing, whatever, whenever the coast ever raised, I'd see the two guys get a raise too. So once the coast got seven and a half cents raised and we got nothing, then we changed with the CIO.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what did it? Is that what did it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He brought the leaders of the Fabel, he got them in the main office, gave them a nice job, and we were there. So, a guy from Carson, Trump, you know, he was a radical guy too, very smart, oh, he was a court date, hey, they called him communist and everything else, but he was a hundred. I don't give a damn what he was, he was a hundred. And we organized, good union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the main organizer?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, he wasn't, no, of course, they came from outside to help us organize.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did this guy, what was his name?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Chauncey Noel, Chauncey Noel, yeah, he died too, in a couple of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what did he do? Was he the leader?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, he was used to working down there, same as anybody else. But he was kind of a leader. But boy, when he started in, he was for the, he'd go to hell anytime for a guy, you know. If he had a vision, boy, he had the guts to go clear up to the top, the man, if he had to, see, he wasn't afraid to fight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he become the head of the local, Chauncey Noel?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, he was.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: So he really believed in the men, I mean, he was on your side, there was no question about it.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He was for the underdog.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Did he talk much about unionism, and about, I mean, you say they called him a radical, he must have been pretty outspoken then. Well, he was, he was, he was in the, let's see, the guy is still alive, he's a lawyer, to start with, but he was, but they made him a vice president, a beard more, Daniel, the guy was in Beardmore, he was a, he was a, him and two other guys, but he was doing all the talk, he was representing the company all the time, you know.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, and boy, him and this Noel, they knocked a horn many times, you know, even when I was there. But then, when Chauncey Noel died,

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: they said this, this beardmore was a poor bear. And he came out in the paper and said, how did he say, he said, the guy, the guy called me a son of a bitch once, and he said that I was a poor bear at his funeral. Yeah, he admitted that the guy was a radical and all that, son of a bitch, but he said, but he was, but the guy said, you couldn't buy him out. Any kind of respect.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard to beat the AF of L and get the new union?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, to beat them, it was nothing but a bull trip. When all the members found out that what they had done, you see, so the liberal board came in and held an election and we got the CIO, that's all it was to it. And then of course, the people from Portland or something, they come over here and started out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When there was a strike, the strike, the first strike that you were in here, was there ever a strike at Elk River in the mill? No, no, no, no, what the hell are you equal to?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could you strike up an elk?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I want to tell them this story.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: We had the, it was Hoover, you heard about Hoover?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Okay, okay, then here comes the shots the goddamn plants done and everybody back in the, and the, and the goddamn, Superintendent come out and be sure to vote for Hoover, we want the sawmill to run next Monday, you know. And one guy said, oh shit, you know, but one guy heard him say that, yeah, he said it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear what he said?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He said, you better vote for Hoover. He said, I bought a house in this town. He said, I want this goddamn sawmill to run.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: So you see, the general over here, if I went, say it happened, oh gosh, I don't know how many times, it happened, every time it was an election, they shot the whole plant down, and Ted was a Republican, what else?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Over here too, your Roosevelt outlawed that. But over here, it couldn't belong to a union, or you had to be a Republican. Now.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: He's a Republican. Jesus Christ.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Republican or else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was a strike, the strike here, was that in 36 that they had that strike?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I don't remember, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They shut that, this mill, didn't they shut the mill down here, or did they try to?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: When they, when they, when they, what about the strike, you know, they called the strike and the company locked the gate, see, for two weeks or something like that, you know, and it was in the fall of the year, and then they, they started, they got two of those suckers, you know, who, who, what the hell would you call them?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: The company who bought them out and they started, they go back to, you know, going back to work and all that stuff. Well, hell yeah, lost the strike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't they have somebody like, your name was Potlatch Rose or something?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah, oh yeah, oh yeah.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I called that, I called that gal little bitches of them many times, she got to know me, you know something?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh, probably, oh, maybe eight or ten years after she was down there, night, she was, you know, anytime you, something, you had to go to her, you know, if you want to leave off or get sick. One night, by gosh, I, I got a kink on my back, I went over to see her, she, she, died me down, oh no, no, no, no, don't, don't worry, and slept until time to come home and got paid for, she, she treated me just like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, but, well, didn't they, like, didn't they kind of bring her in to try to get the men to go back to work, wasn't that part of the job?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I told you, there's a bunch of those guys, just like her and her husband, you know, she was a leader, and few more of those guys, you know, got them in, they gave them free pop, free lunch, free show and all that stuff. And then they go back to work. Well, I tell you, the money, money, look, next to him, not with his money, not with his money, with the, with the, people, when they want to elect him, as crooked as he was, proving crooked, the first time he ran for vice, ran with Eisenhower, he, he, he was supposed to be impeached. Pictures in the paper, on all the magazines in the country, you know, and then get elected, wife, they bought, both, spending 78 million dollars.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yes, crooked is the day, is the day is long. And, they never told us, but I finally got it, and I got a little paper, I got to hang it in some place. He ended up with seven homes, five, two in California, and five in Florida. He sold two already.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, I want to ask you a little more about Elk River, when you were there, most of your friends, the people that you spent your time with, were they Italian, or were they people?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, no, no, I, no, I, I didn't have any Italian friends anymore than I, in fact, I made, I made friends with, with other people, Swedes and what have you, Norwegian, you see, my first wife was Norwegian, and then I got no friends. No, no, no, no, plain American people. No, I, I got away from Italian people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any reason, or did it, was it easy, did that make it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, I was only from the south, it was the same way. So, nobody talked my language, I might as well talk English then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, what about the bootlegging in those days?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Wasn't there a lot of bootlegging, and, and, and, well, yeah, one, one, one, one Monday morning, I and my brother were coming home for lunch. We jumped the fence, and we had probably half a mile walk in the railroad, a good walk in the railroad, kind of plowed out. There's no bank that high, both sides, and all at once we seen something sticking out, put up the bottle, pop, it was one of the pop bottles for the, uh, moonshine. God damn it, we picked it up probably fifteen or twenty. They were put in there on Saturday night for the dance, you see, and the guy that was bootlegging probably got drunk and forgot him, and they all couldn't find him, so, we, we, we went back in there, with a gunnysack, and got him, and then we sold him. That's it. That's how much bootlegging I done. Because no, they didn't drink, and I didn't, no, I, I didn't touch it.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I would like to drink some beer, oh yeah, beer, why not?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Not to whisk you. Now I'll take a drink whisking you, and the doctor won't let me. Well, when, uh, in most, then, was there much drinking in the, in the, around the mill, and the, uh, and the, uh, No, not in the mill, because they can you, they can you, you know, but there was a few of those guys that, uh, they would drink, start drinking Saturday night, drink until Sunday night, you know, and then sometime they, they come on the job not drunk, but sick. Yeah, that, that, I've seen none of that. In fact, there's one guy right now, he's been having a hell of a time, you know, operation, and that's his only, to kill all the N .I .M., and, and he just, uh, had one foot in the grave for a long time.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Abused himself, you know. So there's some guys that did that. They'd be married men too, it wouldn't matter.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the, what about the gambling? Was there much of that?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, gambling, it was nothing, nothing, uh, say a few friends get together, there was nobody getting any record, you know. No, no, nobody was around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure, but then like, then like there's a lot of guys playing pool, didn't they, in downtown and in the river?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, pool of course, but there were no money, no money back from pool.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah. No, eh, but there was, like I said, two or three pool halls, and they'd go through, and play cards, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that guy? Harry Adams had one of those pool halls?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Jesus Christ, yes, I knew him so well.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: His son is the mayor of his house. Yeah, yeah, that's what, yeah, that's right. I heard Harry was quite a, really knew how to tell a story. Oh Jesus Christ.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, great liar, huh?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Once, you know, he was a baller head. He was a baller head, and, eh, eh, that was the first, eh, the year before I came to Al Criven. He had a pool hall right in the corner, and everybody going to work and stuff, you know. And old Harry had this baller head, and, eh, one guy came along, he was going to work, got a minor, even though he felt his head. My God, he said, I feel like my wife is ass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Old Harry, hmm, my God, he said, it does, doesn't it?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Jesus Christ, the guy got mad, they called him huff, he's, a lot of kids, he's going to be true, God damn. Old Harry, he just went and left, he didn't want to hear it.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Then when the thing got rough, you know, he would say, I'm not afraid of any damn man, and goddamn few women. Oh, you're a clinical man. I like him, he's nice.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Well, eh, well, for Dr. Sharp, I'm supposed to eat my lunch, if you want some more information, I'll be over here in ten minutes. Well, maybe I should get going, I suppose see somebody this afternoon. But maybe I'll try to stop by again another time. Okay, that's fine, that's fine, I'll be around. If I can, I will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The only thing I was going to ask you was, what the difference, when you got up to get up, you know, I mean you got to move up, was it in the lumber grading?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference, at Elk River you had a low job?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: No, no, no, over here, it was over here, I didn't grade in Elk River, over here, started grading over here. And there was so many, so finally I got to be one of the top and I was running, that's why I don't hear anymore, around one of the biggest machines they had, running all those heavy shops, big, both that wide and that thick, you know, or it was just, even cotton in here wouldn't help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it in the planer?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: In the planer, oh yeah, right next to the planer there you see grading. And that was it. But then, you know, even then, they bought all of that, this path, you know, and once I was supposed to go up again another nickel an hour, what they call a replant, and he passed me up and I never said nothing, I could hold it but I didn't. Well, I'm okay, oh yeah, it's okay. And he didn't. So something came up two, three months afterwards and I don't remember just exactly where it went, finally then I nailed him down.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I said how come he passed me up two, three months ago?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: I don't know why. So I went over to the union right away. I said, you know, told the union what he done, I told the steward, and right away the Bauhausa Jesus Christ said, well I'll fix it up. So a job opened him up here to a little sawmill four miles from here. They were buying all the lumber at the sawmill of Katte. He sent me up there to check out the lumber. Telling and grading it and I was getting a nickel an hour more and a half hour over time for making out my telly sheet and held on to the wood to get away from all that. But he had to do it, otherwise he wouldn't have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's what a union does for you, right?

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: That's right, that's right.

speaker: Pete Paolini
words: If the union wasn't any good though, how come the lawyer, the dancers, the undertaker, the preacher, how come they belong to the union?

words: Coming, coming, coming. I was wondering why the werden's come and sit in the house while his company's coming. Well we just got talking out here.
